questions.question: A converging lens is:
options: Thinner at the center than at the edges
Thicker at the center than at the edges
questions.explanation: A converging (convex) lens bulges outward and is thicker in the middle, causing parallel rays to converge.

questions.question: When parallel rays pass through a converging lens, they:
options: Diverge as if from a point
questions.explanation: A convex lens brings parallel rays together at a single point — the focal point.

questions.question: When an object is placed beyond $2F$ of a converging convex lens, the image formed is located:
options: Between $F$ and $2F$, real, inverted, and diminished.
Beyond the position $2F$, real, inverted, and magnified.
At the exact position $F$, real, inverted, and same size.
Inside the position $F$, virtual, upright, and magnified.
questions.explanation: When an object is located beyond $2F$ of a convex lens, the refracted light rays converge on the opposite side of the lens within the interval between the principal focus ($F$) and twice the focal length ($2F$). This produces an image that is real, inverted, and smaller than the original object.

questions.question: When an object is placed at $2F$ of a convex lens, the image is:
options: At $2F$, real, inverted, and same size
Behind the lens, virtual, upright, and magnified
questions.explanation: At $2F$, the image forms at $2F$ on the opposite side — real, inverted, and the same size as the object.

questions.question: When an object is placed between the lens and its focal point (inside $F$), the image is:
questions.explanation: Inside $F$, the convex lens acts as a magnifying glass — virtual, upright, and magnified image on the same side as the object.

questions.question: A convex lens is used as a magnifying glass when the object is placed:
questions.explanation: The magnifying glass effect occurs when the object is inside the focal point, producing an enlarged virtual image.

questions.question: In a camera, the convex lens forms an image that is:
questions.explanation: The object is far away ($> 2F$), so the image on the film or sensor is real, inverted, and diminished.

questions.question: A projector uses a convex lens. The object (slide) is placed:
questions.explanation: Between $F$ and $2F$, the lens produces a real, magnified, inverted image — ideal for projecting onto a distant screen.

questions.question: The focal point of a converging lens is defined as the location where:
options: Incoming parallel rays converge following refraction.
The physical glass profile achieves maximum thickness.
An object must sit to project same-size images.
Refracted wave paths diverge into virtual patterns.
questions.explanation: By definition, the principal focal point of a converging convex lens is the specific location on the principal axis where light rays that enter the lens parallel to one another converge and intersect after passing through the glass.

questions.question: If an object is placed at the focal point of a convex lens, the image is formed:
questions.explanation: Rays from an object at $F$ emerge parallel. They never converge to form an image — the image is at infinity.

questions.question: A ray passing through the optical center of a lens:
options: Bends away from the principal axis
questions.explanation: The optical center is the point where incident and emergent rays are parallel — a ray through it is undeviated.

questions.question: For a convex lens to produce a real image, the object must be placed:
options: At or beyond the focal point
questions.explanation: Real images form when the object is at or beyond $F$. Inside $F$, the image is virtual.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered converging lenses and image formation!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review the image characteristics when the object is at $2F$ and beyond $2F$.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on the five object positions and the resulting image properties.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: convex lens shape, focal point, real vs. virtual images, and ray diagrams.
